summary: Joel Spolsky shares an excerpt from his 'Field Guide to Developers,' part of his Guerrilla Guide to Hiring series. The post highlights the near-impossibility of securing private offices for software developers due to accumulated organizational excuses. He argues that office layout decisions are typically made by people far removed from development work, with virtually no input from the developers themselves. The post serves as a teaser linking to the full article on what developers want in a workplace.
key_points: Private offices for developers are nearly impossible to obtain even at well-run companies due to accumulated organizational excuses
Office layout decisions are made infrequently (once a decade) by people with no understanding of developer productivity needs
Architecture firms promote open-office ideology disconnected from the realities of knowledge work
Developers themselves have close to zero input on workspace decisions that directly affect their productivity
money_quotes: A number of excuses all pile up until it's virtually impossible to get private offices for developers in any but the most enlightened of companies.

The decision of where to move and where people should work is often taken once every ten years by a committee consisting of the office manager's secretary and a junior associate from a big architecture firm.
A junior associate from a big architecture firm, who is apt to believe architecture-school fairy tales about how open spaces mean open companies.
Close to zero input from the developers or the development team.
key_insight: Developers' workspace needs are systematically ignored because office layout decisions are controlled by people who don't understand or consult with the people who actually do the work.
